story:
People avoid me on sight, so I keep to my cottage in the “bewitched” forest. When a young man knocks, I’m startled—and hopeful. Business has been slow. I step outside, pull back my hood, flash my shining blue eyes, and make my pitch: “Who can I resurrect for you today?” His face blanches, heart probably thudding like a trapped bird. Ah. Not here for my services, then.

Yes, I’m a necromancer. Taboo, I know. But it’s a talent I stumbled into, not a choice. I get a thrill raising someone from the dead, and for a heartbeat my customers glow when the body rises—until they realize their loved one is scabby, rotting, not themselves at all. If that shocks them, well, imagine being buried. After the corpse stands, it isn’t my problem. If anyone’s to blame, it’s the one who asked for it. Thick skin is required in this line of work. “Ten gold and five silver,” I tell him anyway. “Pricey, yes, business has been slow.” He shakes his head. I sigh. Lost traveler, frightened by the word necromancer. Figures.

I hover in the doorway, wary of knives and righteous fury, then wave him in. “It’s a cottage, not a scary fortress full of rattling skeletons.” The words feel good; it’s been ages since I had a guest. “Mind your step, and ignore the jars.” Pickled fingers—tools from when I was learning, more souvenir than sinister. I send him to the main room, promise lemon water, then remember I have no lemons. Plain water it is. The jug is dusty; I haven’t shared a drink in forever. Bread and butter will make me hospitable, won’t it?

He’s cowering in the chair, so I shake the jug and offer a friendly smile. He forces one back. I hate false gestures but keep cool. “Your name?” “Sandy.” “I’m Arian,” I say, “famous around here, apparently.” He didn’t know this was “my” forest. It isn’t—I just live where others won’t have me. Silence settles, the kind I know too well. I pour water. He eyes it like poison.

“What’s your profession?” “Carpenter.” I clap, genuinely delighted, sloshing my drink. “This table—good quality?” He nods, because who argues with a necromancer? I thrust a plate. He won’t eat; I chew too fast, then too slow, torn between sending him away and keeping him here. Balance is tricky. My teeth meet my fingers—ow. Blood. I laugh it off, call myself vain, ask if I look fat. He shakes his head and half rises. “Words, not just nods,” I scold, and press him back into the chair. “Tell me what you think of my table.”

He mutters. I can’t hear. My grip tightens. He slips, thuds to the floor. Is this what they expect—me, the violent monster? My boot hovers, then I turn and punch the wall instead. Pain blooms. Breath steadies. “It’s been a pleasure,” I manage. “Best you go back the way you came.”

Please stay, say something kind. He bolts. Of course he does.

I flex numb hands and pull up my hood. It shields me, makes me feel human again, unseen. Bitter silence returns. I tell myself I’m not a killer. I am the middle hand between life and death. I give life, in a way, and the mindless corpse is not my cruelty but the cost of a wish. Still, to be what I am is to be alone.
